sections.title: Shock Management
sections.content:
Shock is a life-threatening condition where the body is not getting enough blood flow, leading to a lack of oxygen to vital organs. Immediate first aid can save a life.

Shock is always a medical emergency. Even if the person seems to recover, they still need urgent medical evaluation.

Call your local emergency number immediately (e.g., 911/112/999).

steps.title: Recognize Shock Signs
steps.subtitle: Pale, cold, clammy skin, Rapid, weak pulse, Fast, shallow breathing, Dizziness or fainting, Nausea or vomiting, Thirst, Confusion or restlessness, Bluish lips or fingernails (late sign).

steps.title: Position Patient
steps.subtitle: Place them on their back, elevate the legs about 6–12 inches (unless you suspect head, neck, spine, hip, or leg injury).

steps.title: Control Bleeding
steps.subtitle: Stop any visible bleeding immediately by applying firm direct pressure with a clean cloth or bandage. Use a tourniquet only if trained and bleeding is severe.

steps.title: Maintain Airway
steps.subtitle: Ensure open airway, provide oxygen if available.

steps.title: Prevent Heat Loss
steps.subtitle: Cover patient with blankets, maintain normal body temperature.

steps.title: Monitor Vitals
steps.subtitle: Continuously monitor pulse, breathing, and level of consciousness.
